sections.heading: Why Localization and Accessibility Matter
sections.content: Localization (l10n) is the process of adapting your app for different languages, regions, and cultures — allowing you to reach a global audience. Accessibility (a11y) ensures your app is usable by people with disabilities including visual, motor, auditory, and cognitive impairments. Both are first-class concerns on Android: the platform provides built-in APIs and tools for string translation, RTL layout support, plurals, date and number formatting, TalkBack screen reader support, and more. Localizing your app increases your potential user base dramatically — over 70% of Google Play downloads come from outside the United States. Accessibility compliance is not only ethically important but increasingly a legal requirement in many markets, and Google Play highlights accessible apps in search rankings.

rows.definition: Designing and building your app so it can be adapted to different locales without engineering changes
rows.examples: Externalizing all strings, using locale-aware date/number formatters, supporting RTL layouts, avoiding hardcoded text in layouts

rows.definition: The actual process of adapting the app for a specific locale — translating strings, providing locale-specific resources
rows.examples: Providing res/values-fr/ for French, res/values-ar/ for Arabic, locale-specific images or icons

rows.definition: A combination of language and region that determines formatting and translation — e.g. en-US, fr-FR, ar-SA
rows.examples: en-US (English, United States), zh-CN (Chinese Simplified, China), pt-BR (Portuguese, Brazil)

rows.definition: Text direction used by Arabic, Hebrew, Farsi, and Urdu — layouts must mirror horizontally for RTL locales
rows.examples: Navigation drawer on the right, back arrow pointing right, text aligned to the right

sections.heading: Externalizing Strings
items: All user-visible text must be defined in res/values/strings.xml — never hardcode strings in layouts or Kotlin/Java code
Reference in XML layouts: android:text="@string/welcome_message"
Reference in Kotlin: getString(R.string.welcome_message) or context.getString(R.string.welcome_message)
String with argument placeholders: <string name="greeting">Hello, %1$s!</string> — use getString(R.string.greeting, userName)
Use %1$s for string, %1$d for integer, %1$f for float — numbered arguments allow translators to reorder them
Avoid string concatenation: never do getString(R.string.hello) + name — use placeholders instead so translators can reorder
Mark strings that should NOT be translated: tools:ignore="TypographyDashes" or translatable="false": <string name="app_id" translatable="false">com.example.app</string>
Use the Translations Editor in Android Studio (open strings.xml > Open editor) to manage all locales in one view

rows.description: Default strings — used as fallback when no locale-specific resource matches
French language, Canada region — more specific than values-fr/
Arabic language strings — also triggers RTL layout by default
Dark mode resource overrides — combined with locale: values-fr-night/
Locale-specific drawables — e.g. images with embedded text that need translation
RTL-specific layouts — only needed for complex RTL overrides; prefer start/end attributes instead

sections.heading: Plural Strings
items: Use <plurals> in strings.xml for quantities that have different forms in different languages
English has two forms (one, other) but other languages may have up to six (zero, one, two, few, many, other)
Declare: <plurals name="message_count"><item quantity="one">%1$d message</item><item quantity="other">%1$d messages</item></plurals>
Use in Kotlin: resources.getQuantityString(R.plurals.message_count, count, count) — first count selects the plural form, second is the format argument
Never hardcode plural logic in code with if (count == 1) — always use <plurals> so translators can define correct forms
Arabic has six plural forms — always provide all relevant quantity items for languages you support
Russian, Polish, and Czech have complex plural rules — trust the plural system rather than coding rules manually
Test plurals with different values: 0, 1, 2, 5, 11, 21 to ensure correct forms are selected

rows.description: Add android:supportsRtl="true" to <application> in AndroidManifest.xml — required for RTL mirroring
Replace layout_marginLeft with layout_marginStart, layout_marginRight with layout_marginEnd — automatically mirrors in RTL
Use gravity="start" instead of gravity="left" — resolves to left in LTR and right in RTL
Use app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf and End constraints — avoid Left/Right constraint attributes
Mark drawables that should flip horizontally in RTL with android:autoMirrored="true" in the vector drawable
rows.example: <vector android:autoMirrored="true"> — for back arrows, forward chevrons, directional icons

rows.description: Test RTL layout without changing device language via Developer Options or adb
rows.example: adb shell settings put global debug.force_rtl 1 — forces RTL layout for all apps

sections.heading: Locale-Aware Formatting
items: Never hardcode date, time, number, or currency formats — they vary significantly across locales
Format dates with DateFormat.getDateInstance(DateFormat.MEDIUM, locale) or DateTimeFormatter.ofLocalizedDate(FormatStyle.MEDIUM).withLocale(locale)
Format numbers with NumberFormat.getInstance(locale) — handles decimal separators (1,234.56 vs 1.234,56)
Format currency with NumberFormat.getCurrencyInstance(locale) — handles symbol, position, and decimal format
Use android.text.format.DateUtils for relative time strings ('5 minutes ago') — automatically localized
Avoid String.format() for numbers — use NumberFormat instead to respect locale decimal and grouping separators
Use Locale.getDefault() to get the current device locale — pass it explicitly to formatters rather than relying on system default
For Compose: use LocalContext.current and stringResource() / pluralStringResource() for locale-aware string access

sections.heading: App-Level Language Selection (API 33+)
sections.content: Since Android 13 (API 33), users can set a per-app language preference directly in system Settings > Apps > Language, independent of the system language. This is handled by the Android platform through the LocaleManager API. Jetpack's AppCompatDelegate provides a backward-compatible API for supporting per-app language selection down to API 14. When the user selects a language for your app, the system recreates the app with the new locale configuration automatically. You should declare your supported locales in res/xml/locales_config.xml so the system knows which languages your app supports and can display them in the language picker.

sections.heading: Per-App Language Configuration
items: Declare supported locales in res/xml/locales_config.xml: <locale-config xmlns:android="..."><locale android:name="en"/><locale android:name="fr"/><locale android:name="ar"/></locale-config>
Reference in manifest: <application android:localeConfig="@xml/locales_config">
Set app language programmatically (API 33+): getSystemService(LocaleManager::class.java).applicationLocales = LocaleList.forLanguageTags("fr")
Jetpack backward-compatible API (API 14+): AppCompatDelegate.setApplicationLocales(LocaleListCompat.forLanguageTags("fr"))
Get current app locale: AppCompatDelegate.getApplicationLocales() — returns LocaleListCompat
Reset to system language: AppCompatDelegate.setApplicationLocales(LocaleListCompat.getEmptyLocaleList())
Add Appcompat dependency for backward compat: implementation("androidx.appcompat:appcompat:1.6.x")
The system automatically shows your app in the system language picker if locales_config.xml is declared

rows.description: Android's built-in screen reader — reads UI elements aloud for users with visual impairments; navigates via swipe gestures
rows.developer_action: Ensure all interactive and informative views have meaningful content descriptions

rows.description: Text description of a view read aloud by TalkBack — required for ImageViews, ImageButtons, and icon-only views
rows.developer_action: Set android:contentDescription="@string/profile_photo_desc" on all non-text visual elements

rows.description: Interactive elements must be at least 48x48dp — smaller targets are hard to tap for motor-impaired users
rows.developer_action: Use minWidth/minHeight of 48dp or wrap in a container with sufficient padding

rows.description: Text must have sufficient contrast against its background — WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text
rows.developer_action: Use Accessibility Scanner or Android Studio lint to check contrast ratios

rows.description: The order in which TalkBack moves focus between elements — should be logical and follow reading order
rows.developer_action: Use android:accessibilityTraversalAfter/Before or nextFocusDown/Up/Left/Right to define focus order

rows.description: Navigation using external switches instead of touch — for users with motor impairments
rows.developer_action: Ensure all interactive elements are focusable and reachable via keyboard/switch navigation

sections.heading: Content Descriptions
items: Set content descriptions on ImageViews: android:contentDescription="@string/send_button_description"
Set in Kotlin: imageView.contentDescription = getString(R.string.send_button_description)
For decorative images that convey no information, mark as not important for accessibility: android:importantForAccessibility="no"
In Compose: use semantics { contentDescription = "..." } modifier or Modifier.semantics { }
For ImageButtons and FABs, the content description IS the accessible label — make it action-oriented: 'Send message' not 'envelope icon'
Don't describe the type of control in the content description — TalkBack already announces 'button', 'image' etc.
Group related views with android:screenReaderFocusable="true" on the container so TalkBack reads them together
Dynamic content descriptions should update when the view state changes — e.g. 'Play' vs 'Pause' for a media button

rows.purpose: Text read by TalkBack for non-text views
Control whether a view is exposed to accessibility services
Associates a TextView label with an EditText — TalkBack reads the label when the EditText is focused
Placeholder text in EditText — read by TalkBack when the field is empty
Announces dynamic content changes automatically without user focus
rows.example: android:accessibilityLiveRegion="polite" for non-urgent updates, "assertive" for urgent

rows.purpose: Makes a container focusable as a single unit by screen readers
rows.example: android:screenReaderFocusable="true" on a card view grouping related content

rows.purpose: Makes a non-interactive view focusable for keyboard and switch access navigation
rows.example: android:focusable="true" on a custom view that handles click events

sections.heading: Accessibility in Jetpack Compose
items: Compose uses semantics properties to expose information to accessibility services
Add content description: Modifier.semantics { contentDescription = getString(R.string.send_desc) }
Merge semantics for grouped elements: Modifier.semantics(mergeDescendants = true) { } — reads all child text as one unit
Mark decorative elements: Modifier.semantics { invisibleToUser() }
Custom role: Modifier.semantics { role = Role.Button } — announces the correct control type to TalkBack
State description for toggle elements: Modifier.semantics { stateDescription = if (checked) "On" else "Off" }
Live region equivalent: Modifier.semantics { liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Polite }
Test accessibility in Compose: use composeTestRule.onNodeWithContentDescription() in UI tests to verify semantic labels
Use AccessibilityChecks from Espresso Accessibility Checks library to automatically detect accessibility issues in tests

rows.how_to_use: Enable in Settings > Accessibility > TalkBack — navigate your app using swipe gestures; verify all elements are announced correctly
Install from Play Store; tap the scan button to get suggestions for contrast, touch targets, and missing labels on the current screen
Tools > Layout Inspector — inspect semantic properties and content descriptions on live running apps
Android Studio lint warns about missing contentDescription, low contrast, and other accessibility issues at build time
AccessibilityChecks.enable() in your Espresso test setup — automatically runs accessibility checks on every Espresso interaction
Enable in Settings > Accessibility > Switch Access — verify all interactive elements are reachable and operable via switch navigation

sections.heading: Localization & Accessibility Checklist
items: All user-visible strings externalized to strings.xml — no hardcoded text in layouts or code
Plural strings use <plurals> — no hardcoded singular/plural logic in code
All layouts use start/end instead of left/right attributes
android:supportsRtl="true" declared in the manifest
RTL layout tested using Developer Options force RTL setting
Dates, numbers, and currencies formatted using locale-aware APIs
All ImageViews and icon-only buttons have meaningful content descriptions
Decorative images marked with importantForAccessibility="no"
All touch targets are at least 48x48dp
Text contrast ratio meets WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for normal text)
App tested with TalkBack enabled — all screens navigable and understandable
Supported locales declared in res/xml/locales_config.xml (API 33+)
Translations reviewed by native speakers — not just machine translated

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Localization and accessibility are essential for building an inclusive, globally competitive Android app. For localization, externalize all strings to strings.xml using placeholders and <plurals> for quantities, use locale-aware APIs for formatting dates, numbers, and currencies, and support RTL layouts by replacing left/right attributes with start/end throughout your layouts. Declare supported locales in locales_config.xml to enable per-app language selection on Android 13+. For accessibility, add meaningful content descriptions to all non-text visual elements, ensure touch targets are at least 48x48dp, maintain sufficient color contrast, and use semantics in Compose for full screen reader support. Test with TalkBack, Accessibility Scanner, and Espresso Accessibility Checks to catch issues before they reach users. Both localization and accessibility are investments that expand your audience and improve the experience for all users.
